coredata.dc:title: B-doped GQD supported cobalt sulfide nanocomposite: A defect engineering approach for superior oxygen electrode performance

coredata.dc:description: Developing an efficient and durable electrocatalysts for oxygen electrolysis is crucial for advancing clean energy technologies. However, the sluggish kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), along with catalyst degradation, remain major obstacles. Here, we optimized the composition of composite nanocatalysts obtained by doping of an electron deficient, B-atoms into graphene quantum dots (GQD) attached with Cobalt Sulfide (CoS) nanostructures. Optimizing the surface structure and investigating the interfacial interactions, the catalyst demonstrated an exceptional oxygen electrode reaction performance. The faster electronic synergism between the defect engineering BGQD and CoS offers more catalytic active sites as well as faster electrical conductivity and higher adsorption/desorption rate of oxygenated intermediates at the electrode surface for the electrolysis processes. Among the optimized composite electrode material CSBGQD-13 (CoS/BGQD (1:3)) exhibited high positive onset (Eonset = 1.04 V vs. RHE) and half-wave potential (E1/2 = 0.84 V vs. RHE) with high limiting current density of 7.6 mA/cm2 at 1600 rpm and a reasonable resistance to the MeOH crossover effect during ORR. In addition, our electrocatalyst demonstrated long-term durability and effective OER activity with the lowest Tafel slope of 82 mV/dec among other CSBGQDs and a lower overpotential of 0.27 V vs. RHE at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. Furthermore, the CSBGQD-13 claims excellent dual function electrocatalytic performance towards ORR and OER with a very small ΔE value (only 0.66 V vs. RHE), a higher catalytic current density. Henceforth, for possible fuel cell applications, we believe that this electrode material may provide an understanding of the principles of metal sulfide carbon dots hybrid catalysts.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of hydrothermal synthesis of CSBGQD nanocomposites, (b) XRD plots of CS and CSBGQD-13 with Rietveld Refinement for the calculation of lattice parameters, (c) Williamson-Hall (W-H) plot or uniform deformation model (UDM) of CSBGQD-13 for the calculation of crystal internal strain of the system, (d) Raman spectra of CSBGQD-13 indicates the defect configuration controlled by local electronic interaction introduced by boron doping and interfacial strain.

(a) Low-resolution TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, and (c) Inverse FFT and plot profile of BGQD and CS in CSBGQD-13 revealing the lattice spacing of (100) plane for both CS and BGQD in the nanocomposite obtained from HRTEM image (b), (d) SAED pattern revealing the presence of (102) and (110) planes of CS in CSBGQD-13, (e) TEM-EDS spectra and results of elemental composition present in CSBGQD-13 with ∼ 2 % B-doping. HRSEM images of (f) CS and, (g) CSBGQD-13; (h) Selected grey area HRSEM image of CSBGQD-13 for the elemental mapping of the elements present in composite material which are digitally shown in (i).

(a) XPS full survey scan of CS, BGQD, CSBGQD-13. Corresponding deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) Co 2p, (c) S 2p, (d) B 1 s, (e) C 1 s, and (f) O 1 s obtained from pristine CS, BGQD and CSBGQD-13 nanocomposite.

Electrochemical ORR performances: (a) Comparison of Cyclic Voltammogram (CV) of CS and CSBGQD-13 in N2 atmosphere, (b) Changes in CVs of CSBGQD-13 in saturated 0.1 M KOH at 10 mV/S under O2 and N2 gas flow; (c) Linear Sweep Voltammogram (LSV) of CS, CSBGQD-13 and 10 % - Pt/C at 1600 rpm rotation speed with scan rate of 10 mV/S in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution to reveal the E1/2 values, (d) LSV polarization curve for ORR under different rotational speed of CSBGQD-13 under the identical electrolytic condition; (e) K-L plots of CSBGQD-13 at different potential to calculate number of electron transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface; (f) ORR Tafel plots of CS, CSBGQD-13 and 10 % - Pt/C at the kinetic control region of the LSV curves of (c).

ORR kinetic measurement and probable mechanistic study: (a) K-L plots of CS, CSBGQD-13 and Pt/C at the potential of 0.4 V vs. RHE to calculate kinetic current for each of the electrode, (b) Comparative bar diagram of number of electrons transferred and kinetic current obtained from (a) for CS, CSBGQD-13 and Pt/C electrodes; (c) Graphical presentation of rate constant with number of electrons transfer against the change in potential of the electrode materials; (d) Probable 4OH-/4e- mechanistic pathways for IS-ORR (Iner Sphere ORR); (e) CV curves at different scan rate (10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 120 mV/S) at the non-faradaic region of CSBGQD-13, (f) Linear plots of ∆J/2 vs. scan rate for the electrode materials to calculate double layer capacitance (Cdl); (g) Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy of CS and CSBGQD-13, and inset equivalent circuit device of the fitted Nyquist plots.

ORR stability and durability test: (a) LSV at rotational speed of 1600 rpm of CSBGQD-13 before and after 8000 CV cycle at 10 mV/S (inset: CV of CSBGQD-13 after 8000 cycles of redox reaction); (b) Chronoamperometric stability test for 36 h of CS, CSBGQD-13 and Pt/C at constant potential of 0.5 V; (c) Chronoamperometric MeOH tolerance (3 M in 0.1 M KOH) test CS, CSBGQD-13 and Pt/C; (d) Graphical representation by a bar diagram of some electrochemical parameter (Eonset, E1/2, Tafel slope & ECSA) changes of before and after 8000 CV cycles.

Electrochemical OER performance in 0.1 M KOH of CS, CSBGQD-13 and RuO2: (a) LSV polarization curve at a scan rate of 10 mV/S, side panel: enlarge plots at 10 mA/cm2 current density to get overpotential values; (b) Corresponding Tafel plots at the overpotential region; (c) Chronoamperometric stability test for 36 h performed at constant potential of overpotential; (d) LSV polarization curve of CSBGQD-13 before and after 8000 CV cycle to get the loss of overpotential value; (e) Overall ORR-OER catalytic performance test for CS, CSBGQD-13, 10 % Pt/C and RuO2 for bifunctional activity test.
Comparative Table for CS, CSBGQD-13, Pt/C, RuO2.

head.ce:title: B-doped GQD supported cobalt sulfide nanocomposite: A defect engineering approach for superior oxygen electrode performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing an efficient and durable electrocatalysts for oxygen electrolysis is crucial for advancing clean energy technologies. However, the sluggish kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), along with catalyst degradation, remain major obstacles. Here, we optimized the composition of composite nanocatalysts obtained by doping of an electron deficient, B-atoms into graphene quantum dots (GQD) attached with Cobalt Sulfide (CoS) nanostructures. Optimizing the surface structure and investigating the interfacial interactions, the catalyst demonstrated an exceptional oxygen electrode reaction performance. The faster electronic synergism between the defect engineering BGQD and CoS offers more catalytic active sites as well as faster electrical conductivity and higher adsorption/desorption rate of oxygenated intermediates at the electrode surface for the electrolysis processes. Among the optimized composite electrode material CSBGQD-13 (CoS/BGQD (1:3)) exhibited high positive onset (Eonset = 1.04 V vs. RHE) and half-wave potential (E1/2 = 0.84 V vs. RHE) with high limiting current density of 7.6 mA/cm2 at 1600 rpm and a reasonable resistance to the MeOH crossover effect during ORR. In addition, our electrocatalyst demonstrated long-term durability and effective OER activity with the lowest Tafel slope of 82 mV/dec among other CSBGQDs and a lower overpotential of 0.27 V vs. RHE at a current density of 10 mA/cm2. Furthermore, the CSBGQD-13 claims excellent dual function electrocatalytic performance towards ORR and OER with a very small ΔE value (only 0.66 V vs. RHE), a higher catalytic current density. Henceforth, for possible fuel cell applications, we believe that this electrode material may provide an understanding of the principles of metal sulfide carbon dots hybrid catalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for efficient and sustainable energy conversion technologies has increased due to the world's growing energy consumption and the need to slow down environmental deterioration. In that respect, the developments in clean energy systems are obvious and one such key technology depends on electrochemical reactions such as the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), nitrogen reduction reaction (NRR), and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) etc [1–4]. These reactions possess immense importance mainly in fuel cells, overall water splitting, and nitrogen fixation. Traditionally, noble metals such as Pt, Ru, and Ir have been employed as catalysts for these reactions due to their superior catalytic activities [1,5]. Despite their effectiveness, the high cost, scarcity, and susceptibility to poisoning of these noble metals pose significant challenges to their widespread application [6]. Consequently, there is a compelling need to develop alternative catalysts that are not only cost-effective and abundant but also exhibit high catalytic performance and stability under the operational conditions.

Transition metal chalcogenides have emerged as promising candidates in this context. In particular, cobalt sulfide (CoxSy), is renowned for its rich redox chemistry and intrinsic catalytic activity, making it a potential alternative to noble metal catalysts for reactions such as OER and ORR [6–10]. However, the practical application of CoxSy is impeded by its inherent limitations, including poor electrical conductivity, available of lesser active sites, sluggish kinetics and structural instability during prolonged electrochemical operations [1,11,12]. These drawbacks result in diminished catalytic efficiency and durability, thereby restricting the utility of CoxSy in energy conversion applications. To address these challenges, researchers have explored the integration of CoxSy with carbon-based nanomaterials, aiming to enhance its conductivity and stability [8,10]. Also, Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional derivatives of graphene, have attracted considerable attention due to their unique properties, including quantum confinement and edge effects. These characteristics endow GQDs with exceptional electronic and optical properties, making them suitable for various applications, including catalysis [13–17]. However, the catalytic activity of pristine GQDs is often limited by suboptimal charge transfer kinetics and a scarcity of active sites, necessitating further modification to realize their full potential. A nanohybrid comprising of CoxSy and GQD would certainly be interesting to study to observe any potential synergism in effective energy-related applications [14,16,18].

Furthermore, in the redox processes that deal with oxygen electrode reactions such as ORR at the cathode and OER at the anode side, many oxygen-containing intermediate species/molecules including O*, *OH, OH−, O2 −, HO2 −, H2O2, and *OOH are involved in several adsorption/desorption processes. Often, those intermediates and their reaction steps cause extremely irreversible ORR/OER in aqueous solutions [19–21]. Both the reactions have slow kinetics mainly due to the challenges of acquiring sufficient energy for O2 activation, O–O bond cleavage, OH adsorption/desorption and oxide elimination [7,11]. As a result, the catalysts must meet these stringent requirements. Currently, common methods for improving the activities of benchmark catalyst Pt-metallene include alloying, constructing specific nanostructures with Pt-metallene rich skins, optimizing atom utilization, and enhancing the intrinsic catalytic activity of it by decreasing the size of its’ nanostructures. Nevertheless, throughout the electrochemical process, these fine nanostructures usually have a significant tendency to deform or agglomerate together, which leads to undesirable deactivation and poor stability over time [8,19]. Also, the production of catalysts is expensive due to the intricate synthetic processes. Because of these drawbacks, Pt-based catalysts are still unlikely to be widely used. Thus, the ideal catalyst should be economically feasible, and they must show highly electro-activities, faster kinetics, product selective, stable under operational conditions. In consequence defects in a nanocrystalline domain may induce high distortion of energy and various atomic rearrangements and in turn, it can significantly enhance overall catalytic performances. Additionally, an increase in active-site exposure or modification in their electronic band structure could also be responsible for it [22,23]. Defect engineering is therefore considered as a skillful ability in the catalyst field, especially in electrochemistry [24]. Doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and boron has been identified as an effective strategy to modulate their electronic structure and enhance their catalytic properties [16,17,20,22]. Among these, B doping is particularly noteworthy due to its ability to introduce p-type characteristics, thereby increasing charge carrier density and improving conductivity [15,17,22]. Boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) have demonstrated enhanced catalytic performance in reactions such as ORR, positioning them as promising candidates for energy conversion applications. A nanocomposite with CoxSy-B doped GQDs would envision as potential catalyst for OER and ORR. Despite these advancements, the detailed mechanisms by which B doping in GQDs influence the electrochemical properties of cobalt sulfide composites remain underexplored. Understanding the interaction between B-GQDs and CoxSy is crucial for the rational design of efficient catalysts [8,10,12].

This study aims to bridge this knowledge gap by synthesizing a novel composite material comprising B-GQDs and CoS through a facile, straightforward in-situ hydrothermal method. We have developed a multicomponent–multivalent smart electrocatalytic system that is inexpensive, easy to synthesize, least hazardous and has the advantage of having superior bifunctional activity (OER and ORR). In order to improve catalytic efficiency, selectivity, and stability during the ORR, and OER, our main focus here is on improving appropriate interface structures by increasing the active site density. The integration of B-GQDs with CoS (CSBGQD) is hypothesized to address the limitations of CoS by enhancing its conductivity and stability, thereby improving its catalytic performance in reactions such as OER and ORR.

Cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, extrapure AR, 99 %, SRL), citric acid (C6H7O8, anhydrous, SRL), Boric acid (H3BO3 extrapure AR, 99.5 %, SRL), Potassium hydroxide (KOH, extrapure, 99.5 %, SRL) and Sodium sulfide flakes (Na2S, hydrate, 58–65 %, HIMEDIA) were used without any further purification process. Sustainion® XA-9 alkaline ionomer 5 % in ethanol were purchased from Dioxide Material™ for electrode preparation. The solvent for the entire experiment was de-ionized (DI) water. Ethanol (EtOH) and Methanol (MeOH) were purchased from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd. EtOH, and water mixture was used as solvent for washing and centrifuging purpose. The 10 % Pt/C (99.9 % purity) and RuO2 (99.9 % purity) was bought from Sigma Aldrich and were used for analysis without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CSBGQD with various composition
ce:section.ce:para: An illustration of the synthesis process for CSBGQDs is shown schematically in Fig. 1 a. The metal salt precursors were first dissolved in DI water to get a black precipitate, which was then treated hydrothermally by adding citric acid and boric acid (as a B source for doping). This resulted in preparing in-situ B-doped GQD supported CoS nanocomposites for further ORR/OER activity. Initially, cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 128 mg, 1 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water, forming a 0.05 M Co2+ stock solution. In a separate beaker, sodium sulfide (Na2S, 108 mg, 1.4 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water, forming a 0.07 M Na2S solution. The Na2S solution was then added dropwise into the CoCl2 solution under vigorous stirring, leading to the immediate formation of a black precipitate of cobalt sulfide. To introduce B-GQD, boric acid (H3BO3) and citric acid (C6H8O7) were added in a fixed 3:5 molar ratio, ensuring a controlled doping process. For the CSBGQD-11 (1:1 CoS:BGQD molar ratio), H3BO3 (23.2 mg, 0.37 mmol) and C6H8O7 (120 mg, 0.62 mmol) were dissolved in the reaction mixture. Similarly, for CSBGQD-12 (1:2 CoS:BGQD), H3BO3 (46.4 mg, 0.75 mmol) and C6H8O7 (240.2 mg, 1.25 mmol) were added. In the case of CSBGQD-13 (1:3 CoS:BGQD), H3BO3 (69.5 mg, 1.12 mmol) and C6H8O7 (360 mg, 1.87 mmol) were used, while for CSBGQD-14 (1:4 CoS:BGQD), H3BO3 (92.75 mg, 1.5 mmol) and C6H8O7 (480.30 mg, 2.5 mmol) were employed. Additionally, for the CSBGQD-21 (2:1 CoS:BGQD) composition, the amounts of H3BO3 (11.6 mg, 0.1875 mmol) and C6H8O7 (60 mg, 0.3125 mmol) and for the CSBGQD-31 (3:1 CoS:BGQD) composition, the amounts of H3BO3 (7.7 mg, 0.125 mmol) and C6H8O7 (40 mg, 0.208 mmol) were adjusted accordingly. The final reaction mixture, with a total volume of 40 mL, was stirred for 30 minutes to ensure homogeneous dispersion of precursors before being transferred into a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180°C for 18 hours. After natural cooling to room temperature, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 15 minutes), followed by three sequential washes with a 1:1 (v/v) ethanol-water mixture, and vacuum-dried at 60°C overnight. Control samples were prepared to evaluate the influence of B-GQD integration. Pure CoS was synthesized under identical conditions using a 1:1.4 molar ratio of CoCl2·6H2O (128 mg, 1 mmol) and Na2S (108 mg, 1.4 mmol) without any B-GQD precursors. Similarly, pure B-GQD was synthesized using only H3BO3 (94.5 mg, 1.53 mmol) and C6H8O7 (485 mg, 2.53 mmol) in the absence of CoS. The obtained nanocomposites were labeled as CSBGQD-11, CSBGQD-12, CSBGQD-13, CSBGQD-14, and CSBGQD-21, based on the CoS:BGQD molar ratio used. The synthesis approach ensured the consistent incorporation of boron-doped graphene quantum dots while maintaining the 3:5 molar ratio of boric acid to citric acid, optimizing the electrocatalytic performance of the materials. After vacuum drying at 60°C overnight, the obtained yield for each nanocomposite was measured to assess the reproducibility and efficiency of the synthesis process. The CSBGQD-11, −12, −13, −14, −21, and −31 yielded 24 mg, 36 mg, 56 mg, 68 mg, 52 mg and 57 mg, respectively. Additionally, as reference materials, pure CoS yielded 42 mg, and pure BGQD yielded 29 mg. These values confirm the consistency of the synthesis approach, with yield variations corresponding to the relative precursor concentrations used in each formulation.

Using an Admiral Squidstat Plus electrochemical analyzer with a three-electrode system, all electrochemical measurements were carried out at room temperature. ORR activity was assessed in RDE setup in Pine Wave Vortex® 10 electrode rotator, providing insights into the kinetics and efficiency of the catalytic processes under study using tools like Koutecky-Levich (KL) Plots [23]. The working electrode (WE) was created using the synthesized composite material which was drop-casted on a glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (GC-RDE), and the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl, and the counter electrode was Pt Spring electrode. For each measurement, 2 mg of the composite was dispersed in a 1000 µL mixture of 5 % Sustainion (1:10 v/v with ethanol). This suspension was sonicated for 30 minutes and drop-casted onto the GC-RDE. The area of the WE were calculated approximately 0.0706 cm2 (diameter of the RDE = 3 mm). 25 μL of the prepared stock solution of the catalysts was made to a drop on to the GC-RDE surface and kept for drying at room temperature. The mass loading for each drop casting film was calculated and found to be ∼ 0.71 mg/cm2. For ORR and OER, the benchmark catalysts such as commercially available 10 % Pt/C and RuO2, respectively, were also drop-casted on to RDE surface following same methodology with identical amount of catalyst taken so that the mass loading of the electrode remains same. We have used an O2 saturated 0.1(M) KOH solution as an electrolyte. iR-correction was used to obtain all CV and LSV curves. In order to examine long-term durability, both ORR and OER underwent amperometry stability testing at the E1/2 potentials for the ORR catalysts and at the overpotential for OER to drive the current density at 10 mA/cm2 for 36 hours. The RHE scale has been used to report the measured potential for all electrochemical studies (ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 + 0.059 pH, where E0 Ag/AgCl = 0.197 V saturated with 4 M KCl). Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed at the onset potential of the electrocatalysts with an ac amplitude in the frequency range of 105 Hz to 1 mHz and the performance in EIS for the catalysts have been presented in Table S3. The following formulae determine Cdl from the constant phase element (CPE) components[4]:
(1) Y0 = Q(iω)n
(2) Cdl = Q1/n [1/Rs + 1/Rct](n−1)/n
where, Y0 (Ω−1) is the conductance or capacitance, Q is the CPE constant, n is a constant whose value falls between 0 and 1 (for an ideal capacitor, n = 1), ω is the angle frequency in rad s−1, RS is the solution resistance in Ω, Rct is the equivalent charge transfer resistance in Ω, and i is the imaginary number. To get the surface accessibility of the electrode catalysts electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) has been calculated for ORR/OER using CV at different sweep rate at the non-faradaic potential range. From the slope of the linear plot (Fig. 5 f) of current vs. scan rate obtained from CV curves at the non-faradaic region (Figs. 5 e, S6a-c), Cdl values were calculated. ECSA (Figure S8d) can be obtained from the ratio of Cdl to CS, where CS is equal to 40 μF/cm2 in 0.1 M KOH. ECSA values were then estimated using the Cdl values of the electrodes, and the results showed that CSBGQD-13 had the highest ECSA value (75 m2/g). This indicates that an increase in the specific composition of B-doped GQD with CoS enhanced the active surface site of the catalyst. This results in its intrinsic superior electrochemical activities toward ORR/OER.

The structural parameter was obtained from X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis which was carried at Empyrean Malvern Panalytical Ltd. The XRD measurements were recorded by using Cu Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.54 Å in the 2θ range of 10–90°. Raman spectra of the samples were analyzed using a B&W Tek (USA; BWS415–532H-001) using a 785 nm laser diode. The selected area diffraction pattern (SEAD), morphology, and d spacing were obtained through a JEOL (Japan) JEM-2100 Plus high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) at a voltage of 200 kV. The oxidation state and binding energy of the materials were identified with an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) PHI Versaprobe III using a mono-energetic Al Kα source with an energy of 1486.6 eV to analyse the sample surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements (Fig. 1b and S1) were conducted to establish the crystallographic structures, lattice parameters, and phase integrity of the as prepared pristine CoS (CS), B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQD) and CSBGQD composites. First, the formation of BGQD was confirmed from the presence of (100) and (002) diffraction planes at 14.1 and 27.7° as shown in Figure S1a (XRD of BGQDs alone), which revealed the formation of honeycomb GQD [25,26]. The XRD pattern of pristine CoS exhibits distinct reflections corresponding to its hexagonal phase, with characteristic peaks at approximately 2θ = 30.68°, 35.68°, and 50.39°, associated with the (100), (101), and (110) planes, respectively and aligns with the JCPDS file no. 75–0605 of CoS. The diffraction patterns of CSBGQD nanohybrids (Figure S1b) demonstrate significant changes compared to pristine CS, indicating successful integration of BGQD into the CoS matrix [27,28]. Interestingly, all nanocomposites retained the hexagonal CS phase, as evident from the persistence of the (100), (101), and (110) planes. However, notable differences were observed such as, the (100) peak of CS shifted from 2θ = 30.68° in pristine CS to 31.03° in CSBGQD-13 as shown in Table S1, suggesting lattice distortion due to interaction with BGQD and detail explanation has been provided in Supporting Information. Similar shifts were observed in other compositions, with the degree of shift varying based on the BGQD content indicating optimal interaction between CS and BGQD. The positive slope in W-H (Fig. 1 c) plot for CSBGQD-13 confirms the presence of tensile strain in the lattice. Also, a new diffraction peak at 2θ = 47.45°, corresponding to the (102) plane, appeared in CSBGQD-13, indicating the distortion aroused due to the integration of BGQD matrix [29,30]. This increase suggests reduced defects and enhanced ordering in the composite. This strain likely arises from the mismatch between the CoS lattice and the BGQD matrix which greatly enhances the electrochemical performance by creating more active sites and facilitating charge transfer.

The incorporation of B-doped carbonaceous matrix (BGQD) on CoS was confirmed by the appearance of D and G bands at ∼ 1370 cm−1 and ∼ 1588 cm−1, respectively, in the Raman spectrum, which were absent in bare CoS, as shown in Fig. 1 d. However, the relationship between the intensities of D and G bands (i.e. ID/IG ratio) and the exact peak positions varied slightly among different composite formulations that has been tabulated in Table S2. All the composite and BGQD itself had ID/IG ratios between 0.88 and 0.89, with the exception of composition CSBGQD-13, which displayed 0.94. This composite exhibited a significant degree of disorder or defect concentration in carbon-based materials, as indicated by the increased intensity ratio of the D and G bands for CSBGQD-13 [23]. These differences implied that the incorporation of BGQDs with CoS altered the carbon defect environment in a more typical way [24]. The disorder in the atomic arrangement of C-matrix proves that their degree was not only due to the defect concentration of B-atom in BGQD but also due to CoS and optimum composition of BGQD (i.e. CoS:BGQD = 1:3 (molar ratio), during in-situ synthesis). This emphasized an interesting electronic interaction between the lattice interfaces which thereby enhances the electrical conductivity at the electrode/electrolyte interface during the electrolysis process [23]. In other words, BGQDs did not just introduce additional defects; they appeared to influence how defects were arranged or stabilized at the CoS–BGQD interface. Therefore, the Raman data indicated that the defect configuration in the composite is not random but rather tuned by the local electronic and structural interactions introduced by boron doping and interfacial strain. After analyzing the structural viewpoints by XRD and Raman, we have tested all the electrochemical characterization (CV, LSV and EIS) by CSBGQDs (all the composite material). We found the best composition, i.e. CSBGQD-13 showed the best electrode activity among the others as prepared composite. Hence, in this article we are explaining TEM, and XPS analysis only for CSBGQD-13 for more structural insights.

Furthermore, the structural composition and interfacial interaction between the BGQD and CS in the nanocomposite were clearly identified form the TEM, HRTEM and HRSEM image as shown in Fig. 2,S2 and S3. Figure S2a shows that the control CS particle alone where their shapes were irregular and amorphous in nature, and Figure S2d showed that the BGQD exhibited fairly monodisperse with an average size of about ∼ 4 nm. The lattice spacing from both the HRTEM images were found to be 0.291 nm and 0.209 nm for CS and BGQD respectively, that were nicely corelated with the literature (as shown in Figure S2b, S2e) [31,32]. Figure S2c, S2f represented the SAED pattern of CS and BGQD, where BGQD showed mostly amorphous nature and Figure S2c displays the presence of (110) plane of CS. Co-existence of similar irregular shaped CS with BGQD were confirmed in TEM image in Fig. 2a. In CSBGQD-13, no appreciable changes in their respective lattice spacing were observed in either CS (d = 0.292 nm) or BGQD (d = 0.205 nm) nanostructures [33,34]. This was confirmed through a reverse FFT image construction in Fig. 2 c where the exact value of d-spacing were calculated. The SAED pattern presented in Fig. 2d revealed d-spacing values of 0.192 and 0.171 nm which corresponded to the (102) and (110) planes of CS in CSBGQD-13, respectively. But there is no such crystalline nature for BGQD was observed that was in accordance with the Figure S2f. It was clear that the doping concentration did not vary the macrostructural features in the nanocomposites. EDS data, as shown in Fig. 2e, we observed a 2.2 % atomic doping of B was present in CSBGQD-13. Interestingly, this doping percentage was decreased in the in-situ formation of the nanocomposites, while comparing with pristine BGQD (i.e., 6.1 %), as depicted in Figure S2h. But the atomic ratio of Co:S was obtained ∼ 1:1 for both the pristine CS and composite CS (Figure S2g and Fig. 2e). Therefore, the co-existence of CS and BGQD in CSBGQD-13 nanostructures were confirmed and they were beyond the nature of physical mixture. The pristine CoS in Figure S2a is significantly larger than both BGQD (Figure S2d) and CSBGQD-13 (Fig. 2a). The smaller size of CoS in the composite is likely due to particle nucleation centers that has been inhibited in presence of BGQD precursor during the in-situ process. This is further supported by the HRSEM images of CS and CSBGQD-13 in Figs. 2f and 2g, respectively. The nucleation of CS in the presence of the BGQD precursor also altered the surface structure of the composite, as seen in Fig. 2 g. Figs. 2 h and 2 i confirm the presence of elements in the composite electrocatalyst, while Figure S3 shows the presence of Co and S in CS.

In addition to the structural and chemical composition investigations performed by XRD, Raman and HRTEM, XPS analysis was used to examine the oxidation states and compositional transmutation of the elements contained in the nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 3. Fig. 3a depicts a full-scale survey scan of CS, BGQD, and CSBGQD-13, with additional peaks of B, C, and O in the nanocomposite indicating the integration of BGQD matrix onto CS. The atomic percentages of Co and S in both CS and CSBGQD confirmed the formation of CoS. They also showed that the B atom is doped into CSBGQD-13, reducing from 4 % in BGQD alone to 1.6 % in the composite during the in-situ synthesis. This was in-accordance with our observation in EDS analysis. A minor peak shift in Co 2p for CSBGQD-13 towards higher binding energies (780.5 eV for Co 2p3/2 and 796.8 eV for Co 2p1/2) from CS (780.2 eV for Co 2p3/2 and 796.3 eV for Co 2p1/2) could be clearly perceived from Fig. 3b, suggesting a partial electron donation from cobalt to the BGQD domains [35,36]. Additionally, the satellite peaks in Fig. 3b were indicative of shake-up transitions and confirm spin-orbit splitting, signifying an existence of mixed-valent cobalt environment (Co²⁺/Co³⁺) [37,38]. But Co 2p XPS in both CS and CSBGQD-13, Co2+ appears at a higher binding energy than Co3+ due to a combination of final-state effects, charge screening, and covalency differences. In summary, while XPS directly probes core electrons, shifts in their binding energies serve as indirect evidence of valence electron interactions [37,38]. The observed binding energy shifts

in CoS upon interaction with BGQD indicate changes in electron density, which result from charge transfer from CoS to BGQD and/or screening at the valence level [39,40]. This electron redistribution increased the electrophilicity of cobalt centers, rendering them more active towards oxygen-related intermediates. Such modified electronic environment might directly correlate with the enhanced catalytic performance. Similarly, the shift in S 2p to 162.0 eV and 163.2 eV in CSBGQD-13 from 161.8 eV and 163.0 eV in CS (depicted in Fig. 3 c), indicative of altered sulfur bonding environments likely influenced by boron and oxygen species at the interface with BGQDs [23,24]. These subtle adjustments to the sulfur chemical state can facilitate more efficient electron transfer and improve adsorption/desorption dynamics of reactive intermediates during electrochemical processes. The B 1 s spectrum (in Fig. 3 d) in the composite retains B–C and B–O features, confirming that boron functionalities remain intact and integrated into the CoS matrix [24,41,42]. Preserving these boron-related active sites is essential for maintaining high electronic conductivity and expanding the density of available reaction sites. Meanwhile, the C 1 s signals in Fig. 3 e of CSBGQD continued to reflect graphene-like carbon and oxygenated functional groups from BGQD within the composite. Such functional groups enhance the composite’s interaction with the electrolyte, improve mass transport, and ensure effective reactant access to catalytic sites [23,26]. Finally, the O 1 s peaks at 532.4 eV and 533.1 eV of BGQD was retained in the nanocomposite with a slight deviation towards higher binding energy which can be seen in Fig. 3 f [4,43]. This slight shift in binding energy attributes to the interaction between CS and BGQD matrix. Additionally, the peak aroused at 530.0 eV is due to the coherent interaction between the S of CoS and O from BGQD in the nanocomposite [23]. Thus, taken together; a) induced lattice strain from XRD and HRTEM data, b) defect modulation from Raman analysis, c) the strong interaction between a conductive material with carbon framework, and d) interesting electronic redistribution from XPS data, CSBGQD-13 showed promise as a multifaceted catalyst. We believe the composite capitalized on the best attributes of both components: CoS provides stable, redox-active metal centers, while BGQDs enhance conductivity, defect chemistry, and surface wettability.

Electrochemical ORR study: A rotating disk electrode (RDE) experiment was conducted to examine the electrocatalytic oxygen reduction activity and long-term durability of our as-synthesized catalysts. The electrochemical polarization curves are obtained with the current normalized by the disk area of the glassy carbon electrode. Fig. 4a and S3 showed the results of a comparative CV study at a sweep rate of 10 mV/s for the catalytic ORR activity of pristine

CS and CS/BGQDs nanocomposite in a 0.1 M KOH solution under saturated with N2 gas. It was evident that the area under the CV curve for CSBGQD-13 was found highest among them. The integrated area under the CV curve can directly provide information about the amount of charge transfer during the electrochemical reactions [4,23,24]. Next, the electrochemical change in CV of CSBGQD-13 was studied under O2-saturated KOH medium as shown in Fig. 4b. When comparing the O2-saturated electrolyte to the N2-saturated KOH solution, Fig. 4b showed a clear increase of reduction peak current. In fact, this peak height was higher than that of pristine CS or Pt/C (Figure S5a & S5b, respectively) indicating the superior ORR activity of CSBGQD-13 in comparison to the other two electrodes. More precisely, the sharp increase in cathodic peak current of −1.07 mA/cm2 for CSBGQD-13 was noticed under O2 gas flow while, for the pristine CS and the benchmark catalyst Pt/C, it was found to be −0.04 mA/cm2 and −0.22 mA/cm2, respectively. This increase in reduction peak current in presence of O2, was therefore implies facile ORR activity. Following that, LSV measurements were conducted at a fixed rotating speed of 1600 rpm with scan rate of 10 mV/S to examine the impact of BGQD over CoS on electrocatalytic ORR performance as depicted in Fig. 4c & S6. An electrochemical parameter that is highly helpful in relating the catalysts' active sites and surface reaction efficiencies to the ORR reaction is the halfwave potential (E1/2). Both the onset potential (Eonset) and E1/2 showed an improvement from CS ORR activity following BGQD loading in CS as mentioned in Table 1. The best electrical synergism between the CoS and BGQD interfaces was demonstrated by CSBGQD-13, which has the most positive onset and E1/2 value among all the B-doped GQD supported CoS synthesized so far. Furthermore, from Table 1 and Fig. 4 c the E1/2 is roughly 50 mV lower and the Eonset was more positive of 40 mV for CSBGQD-13 than for Pt/C. These two competing electrochemical parameters made our electrocatalyst highly comparable to the high-cost benchmark catalyst, Pt/C. In consequences with those factors, the high limiting current density (JL) was obtained for CSBGQD-13 in the potential range of 0.6–0 V vs. RHE in the LSV curve (Fig. 4 c). A dip in current profile curve was observed before reaching the limiting current which is attributed to the electrochemical surface phenomena competing between the 2-electron and 4-electron processes at the electrode-electrolyte interface as shown in Figure S7a and S7b for CS and CSBGQD-13, respectively (brief explanation has been mentioned in Supporting Information). The maximum diffusion current density occurred in that potential region during RDE measurement which implied that the maximum concentration of the oxygenated species was being reduced at the electrode surface [4,19,20]. The higher JL for CSBGQD-13 than CS and Pt/C also indicated the greater conductivity at the metal sulfide graphene dot interface and active basal plane of it for significantly enhanced the electrochemical ORR performances. This observation makes our catalyst superior electroactive towards ORR than Pt/C. Accordingly from our findings, bare CS is a poor electrocatalyst for ORR kinetics because of its lower JL, less positive EORR, and Eonset. Also, the superiority of our composite material can be an indicative for an excellent catalyst from Table S4, which compares these good ORR results with previously published literature.

Kinetic behavior of the catalyst during ORR: An excellent method for investigating the oxygen reduction routes in an electrolytic solution is to study the kinetic behavior of the ORR reaction. Thus, in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution, the ORR polarization curves were used to characterize the kinetics of our catalysts' response at various rotation speeds, ranging from 100 rpm to 2500 rpm, at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The limiting current densities of the CSBGQD-13 electrode were shown in Fig. 4d and other catalysts such as CS and 10 % Pt/C (Figure S8a & S8b) showed a sharp rise with increasing rotating speed. This enhanced limiting current density was caused by the catalysts' surface activation and higher adsorption of O2 or oxygenated intermediates, which were triggered by the closer proximity of the electrode and electrolyte [23,35,36]. The number of electron transfer (n) during ORR was also determined from the polarization curves using the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) equation to examine the reaction pathways. This can be explained by the linear plot of the inverse square root of rotational speeds (ω−1/2) against the inverse of current density (j−1), which clearly revealed the first order kinetics for CS, CSBGQD-13 and Pt/C as shown in Fig. 4e, S6c and S6d, respectively. In order to reduce each oxygen molecule efficiently, the number of electrons transmitted for CSBGQD-13 was calculated to be 4.03 (i.e. 4) in the potential range of 0.1–0.6 V vs. RHE (Fig. 4e). This is significantly better than CS (Figure S8c), and even marginally better than commercial Pt/C (Figure S8d). The computed nav values for CS, and commercially available Pt/C were 3.52, and 3.97, respectively, within the same potential range. This indicated that the reduction of O2 to OH- through the formation of H2O2 were more prominent in case of CS electrode than the commercially available benchmark catalyst, Pt/C and CSBGQD-13 (the likely mechanism was given in the Supporting Information) [44,45]. There are two main types of ORR models: a) first-principles-based microkinetic models (i.e., a computational model that uses fundamental quantum mechanics to calculate the kinetics of rate determining step of each elementary steps which are involved in ORR processes) and b) an empirical models based on the Tafel equation (an electrochemically based model that was verified by results from the literature and calibrated using experimental data). Based on empirical models, the more polarization with unfavorable catalytic activity was suggested by a higher Tafel slope, whereas a lower Tafel slope denoted more effective electrocatalytic performance [1,2,21]. With respect to the other as-synthesized composite catalyst (Figure S9), very low Tafel slope (64 mV/dec was observed for CSBGQD-13 (Fig. 4 f), which was comparable to commercial 10 % Pt/C (52 mV/dec). On the other hand, by extrapolating Tafel slope for our best electrocatalyst where ov Thus, excellent kinetic behavior towards ORR was demonstrated. This result reflected a shorter transport path between electrolyte ions and the electrode, demonstrating the most effective catalytic ORR kinetics and offering an enhanced ORR active site.

Kinetics and probable mechanistic study for ORR: In order to elucidate the change in kinetic current (Jk) of BGQD supported CS electrode over CS, at 0.4 V vs. RHE, a linear K-L plot was demonstrated for all the electrodes as shown in Fig. 5a. Jk can be calculated from the intercept of linear plot of J−1 vs. ω−1/2. The first order ORR reaction kinetics were revealed by the linear parallel characteristic of the K-L plots for each electrocatalyst. A bar diagram of electron transfer number during the ORR processes and the kinetic currents of the catalysts were shown in Fig. 5b. It was evident that a specific percentage of composition of defect engineering B-doped GQD supported CS have the highest electron transfer number, the highest Jk value, and a relatively low Tafel slope, indicating that the preference of CSBGQD-13 as

efficient electrode material for fuel cell or metal-air battery applications. In addition to all those electrochemical characteristic parameters, the assessment of the electron transfer rate constant was computed in the potential range of 0.1 V to 0.6 V vs. RHE to verify the enhanced electrocatalytic activity of CSBGQD-13. Compared to CS and Pt/C (Fig. 5 c), the electron transfer rate constant of the composite nanomaterial was found to be substantially faster. The rate constant values for CSBGQD-13 at different potential were nearly three-fold and two-fold greater than those of CS and Pt/C, respectively. Before discussing the experimental data presented herein, first a brief probable mechanism was demonstrated on the accumulation of mobile negative ions near the electrode/electrolyte interface [21]. When the electrode is applied potential, OH- ions were adsorbed on the active sites of the material surface (step I, Fig. 5d). Following the flow of O2 gas during the electrochemical test, O2 molecules collided with the electrode's surface and were subsequently adsorbed (step II), displacing OH- ad. A large number of OH- ions lost their chemical bonding due to that collision. The O2,ad could take the electrons from the electrode material making its surface negatively charged as shown in step III [21,46,47]. The desorbed OH- ions, which were also being negatively charged ions, took part to the hydration of K+ ions as pictorially shown in Fig. 5d. After coming across an area with comparatively low ion conductivity (i.e. into the bulk phase of the electrolyte), the OH- ions were unable to travel towards the electrode surface and then started in the accumulation of mobile negative ions. This effect resulted in a repulsion between the accumulated mobile ions and surface negative charge, which made the OH- ions to migrate away from the electrode surface [4,43,47]. Therefore, the ion conduction at the electrode surface was reduced. Such phenomenon was observed more significantly for CS and Pt/C than CSBGQD-13. The electron transfer was solely from the CS and Pt, making their surface tightly bound to OH- ions, while for CSBGQD-13 an electronic synergism played a significant role in binding OH- ions neither too tightly nor too loosely. In turn, it facilitated the higher ion conduction near the electrode surface. Therefore, the upcoming O2 molecule easily collided with OH- ad and again adsorbed by replacing that OH- ad ions. But in case of CS or Pt/C the surface was primarily blocked with the accumulated negative ions which prevented the collision of O2 molecules with the surface of the electrode sufficiently [48–50]. Hence, a lower Tafel slope and higher electron transfer kinetics for CSBGQD-13 over CS and Pt/C, were witnessed. In the presence of an H2O molecule, during step IV, the adsorbed superoxide (HOO*) formed on the catalyst surface [49]. Subsequently, in step V, an electronic synergism is observed from BGQD to Co(III), leading to the formation of Co(II)S, again. Following an electronic transition, facilitated by the H2O molecule and an electron, the adsorbed O atom was reduced to OH⁻ which was further stabilized within the K-hydration sphere. Importantly, the steps involved in ORR processes are still not fully understood and due to the complexity of this electrochemical process, it is challenging to identify the rate-determining step. However, it was evident that the competitive interplay between the adsorption and desorption of OH⁻ might be the crucial steps. In our case, the kinetics data confirmed that the Inner Sphere Oxygen Reduction Reaction (IS-ORR) mechanism played a critical role in reducing one O₂ molecule to OH⁻ via a 4-electron transfer pathway for CSBGQD-13, thereby establishing it as an efficient electrocatalyst.

Furthermore, in order to investigate the surface properties of the electrodes, electrochemically active surface area (ECSA), which was proportional to double layer capacitance (Cdl), had been computed in Figs. 5e, 5f and S10a-c. The CV technique was used to measure the Cdl values at various scan rates (10–80 mV) throughout the non-Faradic region in the potential range 0.95 – 1.25 V vs. RHE. The CSBGQD-13 composite was shown to have a much larger Cdl (9 mF/cm2) than CS (3.4 mF/cm2) and Pt/C (6.3 mF/cm2). The ECSA values were displayed as a bar diagram in Figure S10d, which implied that our developed composite electrode material offers the highest ECSA. In addition to structural synergism in the electronic nature, an important factor in the improved electrochemical behavior toward ORR was the charge transfer resistance (Rct) obtained from the Nyquist plot of EIS (as displayed in Fig. 5g & S11). The Rct value gradually decreased with increasing amount of BGQD composition and reached a minimum for the CSBGQD-13 (Figure S11 , as shown in Table S3) after the fitting of Nyquist plots by the Randle's equivalent circuit device for electron transfer kinetics [51,52]. The Nyquist plot showed the smallest semicircle for CSBGQD-13 among all as-prepared electrodes with lowest charge Rct of 14.3 Ω supporting its high conductivity and electrical activity compared to CS (133 Ω). The higher electrical conductivity and quicker electron transfer rates at the electrode-electrolyte interface helped in raising the rates of OH-/molecular O2 adsorption. The ECSA and roughness factor (Rf) of the catalysts were improved as the Rct value decreases and the double-layer charge capacitance (Cdl) increases (Table S3). The lower Rct and higher Cdl offered a higher density of accessible active micropores on the electrode surface and strengthened the synergistic interaction between CS and BGQD. Thus, higher ECSA and Rf values are also indicative of the enhanced catalytic processes at the electrode surface.

Electrochemical stability and durability test for ORR: The ORR is a crucial mechanism in fuel cells and metal-air batteries for developing clean sustainable energy technology applications. As such, the stability and durability evaluation of electrode materials are crucial. Since the effectiveness of these devices is limited by the slow kinetics and high overpotential of the ORR, stable and effective electrocatalysts are expected. LSV analysis was used to assess the electrode’s stability and durability both before and after 8000 CV cycles. Although the E1/2 decreased for all the cases, including the 10 % commercial Pt/C (14 mV, Figure S12b), it was encouraging to note that CSBGQD-13 decreased by 17 mV even after 8000 CV cycle ( Fig. 6a). On the other hand, in comparison to CS catalyst (Figure S12a), CSBGQD-13 showed

appreciably low change in E1/2, Eonset as tabulated in Table 1. This suggests that CSBGQD-13 was very stable under such circumstances. In contrast with the durability, the Tafel plots of the electrode materials in the Eonset to E1/2 region following 8000 CV cycles are shown in Figure S13. Thus, it is clear that, with the exception in CS, the catalytic reaction kinetics improved for CSBGQD-13 and Pt/C. The improved kinetics after the long-term cyclic stability of the composite material can be obtained from the electronic synergistic interaction between defect engineering-based B-doped GQD and CS. This interaction made the electrode more stable and active towards ORR performances. Additionally, the chronoamperometric test at the potential of limiting current of each of electrodes (at 0.5 V vs. RHE) were carried out for 36 hours as

shown in Fig. 6b. Interestingly, CSBGQD-13 demonstrated far greater stability, with a lesser drop of current density at constant potential, whereas Pt/C had much more drop. We hypothesized that either Pt nanoparticle aggregated on the amorphous surface of the C moiety or the corrosive nature of Pt during the formation oxide(s)/hydroxide of Pt during O-O bond cleavage in ORR process, might be responsible for the low stability and electrocatalytic activity of Pt/C [53,54]. In addition to the superior durability test, an excellent tolerance towards MeOH poisoning were performed while comparing with CS and Pt/C. As detected during chronoamperometric measurement, shown in Fig. 6c, it was evident that the addition of 3 M MeOH can alter their stability. There was a negligible change in current density for CSBGQD-13, but for CS and Pt/C, a significant change was observed in the current profile during the chronoamperometric test. This is important as a direct MeOH fuel cell experiences methanol oxidation at the cathodic electrode, this MeOH crossover deactivated the ORR activities, and our nanocomposites showed better MeOH tolerance [23,55]. The overall stability, durability and performance change in ORR catalytic function of our cathode material, CSBGQD-13, was shown by a bar diagram in Fig. 6d. Therefore, in comparison to the most advanced Pt/C catalyst, our inexpensive, selective cathode electrocatalyst, CSBGQD-13, exhibited superior ORR catalytic performance, including durability and MeOH tolerance.

Electrochemical OER performances: A three-electrode setup in a 0.1 M KOH solution was used to assess the OER performance of the pristine CS, CSBGQD-13 and commercially available RuO2 (benchmark catalyst for OER) at a scan rate of 10 mV/S. The polarization curves obtained using LSV with the current normalized by the glassy carbon electrode's disk area were displayed in Fig. 7a. The O2 generation was started at about 1.4 V, as seen in Supplementary Figure S14a, and the polarization curve hardly changed during the first ten cycles. From the OER polarization curves, as shown in Fig. 7a and the side enlarge panel of LSV at 10 mA/cm2 current density, indicated that OER performance of CSBGQD-13 was not only significantly better than that of the other electrodes, but also better than that of benchmark RuO2. This revealed CSBGQD-13 as one of the most advanced OER active material among the defect engineering B-doped GQD supported CS. CSBGQD-13 showed the lowest overpotential (η, 270 mV) needed to achieve a current density of 10 mA/cm2 as displayed to Table 1. BGQD supported CS electrode surface worked better to improve electron transfer kinetics, which raises the OER catalytic activity at the electrode surface (mechanism discussed in Supporting Information). Unlike ORR, the OER activity at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, the overpotential and the Tafel kinetics for the electron transfer process were evaluated for all the electrodes. CSBGQD-13 only needed 1.5 V vs RHE, or the overpotential of 270 mV vs RHE, to reach this current. This is 100 mV less than what was needed for commercial RuO2 (1.60 V). In the meantime, pristine CS, CSBGQD-31, 21, 11, 12 and 14 require 430, 340, 460, 322, 436 and 365 mV of overpotential, respectively, to achieve the same current density (Figure S14a). The lower overpotential for CSBGQD-13 indicates the minimum energy required to generate molecular oxygen from OH- at electrode/electrolyte interface. Usually, to explore anodic electrode’s kinetic, Tafel slope was computed at the overpotential region using logarithm of steady-state current density where the smaller slope was the indication of better catalytic reactivities. From Fig. 7b and Figure S14b , it was noticed that CSBGQD-13 showed the lowest Tafel slope of 82 mV/dec in comparison with CS (180 mV/dec), CSBGQD-31 (116 mV/dec), CSBGQD-21 (125 mV/dec), CSBGQD-11 (178 mV/dec), CSBGQD-12 (134 mV/dec), CSBGQD-14 (241 mV/dec) and RuO2 (97 mV/dec). As seen in Fig. 7c, the mass transport activity was supported by establishing the improved OER activity for CSBGQD-13. As seen in the inset bar diagram of Fig. 7c, CSBGQD-13 had a very high mass transport activity that was nearly 9 times more than that of its as-synthesized analog CS at 1.5 V, or η = 270 mV. Hence, the highest mass transport activity of CSBGQD-13 facilitated to amplify the OER activity and renders its best composition of impure B-doped GQD loading on CS [22,52]. Importantly, the most favorable OER kinetics for CSBGQD-13 were also revealed by the lowest Tafel slope, highest exchange current density and maximum mass transport activity, which suggested a quicker charge transport capability and improved electronic conduction between the active sites and the chemisorbed oxygen intermediates (O*). Previous research has proposed an OER mechanism for transition metal sulfides, where an active intermediate might be catalytically produced by the oxidation state of the metal atom present in the metal sulfide as well as calculated exchange current density (Table S5) [56–59]. The conversion of this active intermediate to −OO* species was then initiated by the transfer of 4OH−/4e−, which ultimately dissociated to form O2 molecules. Again, more adsorbed oxygenated intermediates were encouraged by the difference in electronegativity between the Co (1.88), and S (2.58) atoms in CoS. The polarization of the electron distribution and the greater partial charges of the atoms helped in the increasing difference in electronegativity making them more ionic in nature. Since, CSBGQD-13 showed exceptional OER activities, we have shown their better adsorption/desorption of oxygen intermediates through the addition of methanol in the reaction process, as shown in Figure S15. While comparing with bare CS or the benchmark catalyst RuO2, CSBGQD-13 showed suppression of methanol oxidation and faster kinetics towards OER. The negatively charged, electron-rich oxygenated species therefore preferred to be adsorbed on Co due to its greater electropositivity, for better OER activity. Furthermore, the modification of CS with doping of one of the most electron deficient B-atom into GQD facilitated the adsorption of oxygenated intermediate species on the electrode surface [54]. This modification would also alter the electronic energy states within the multicomponent system, offering a higher electronic charge transfer rate (Fig. 5g) and enhanced ECSA (as previously depicted in Fig. 5f, S8c, and S8d). Besides, active surface site, adsorption/desorption of oxygenated intermediates and kinetic current, high exchange current density as compared to other reported materials as show in Table S5 reveals our compositionally varied nanocomposite exhibit one of the superior electrocatalyst towards oxygen electrode reactions. Thus, BGQD helped CS to achieve outstanding electrocatalytic performance by adequately forming the CoS/BGQD heterostructure with a proper proportion of composition. This accelerated redox processes and resulted in lower adsorption energies of the oxygenated intermediate on the electrode surface, making CSBGQD-13 the most superior electrode material for OER processes. Our excellent OER results have been compared with other reported literature and shown in Table S6.

Electrochemical OER stability and durability test: By repeatedly cycling the CV, LSVs of the catalysts-cast RDE after 8000 cycles were performed in the potential range of 1.2–1.9 V vs. RHE to verify the stability and durability of our electrocatalysts for the OER. Chromatoamperometry measurements were performed for 36 hours, at an over potential of corresponding electrode (j = 10 mA/cm2) as illustrated in Fig. 7d. It is clear that the drop of relative current density for CSBGQD-13 at 270 mV overpotential is lower than that of commercial RuO2 at 370 mV overpotential. The retention of current density was 93 % for CSBGQD-13 which was almost comparable with RuO2 during 36 h of chronoamperometric measurement. However, the initial drop in current density for RuO2 was more than that of CSBGQD-13. As the catalyst's active sites gradually deactivated during the electrolysis, strong OER activity typically resulted in structural instability. The rapid deactivation of the active sites in alkaline media resulted in the formation of water-soluble RuO3 2− species [4,48], for decrease OER current density. Besides this chronoamperometric analyses, the results were well justified with the LSV polarization curve obtained after 8000 CV cycles as shown in Fig. 7e, S16a and S16b for CSBGQD-13, CS and RuO2, respectively. The minimum loss in overpotential, i.e., 14 mV was observed for CSBGQD-13 at 10 mA/cm2 and 30 mV for each of CS and RuO2. This signified persistent stability as well as the improved OER activity for a long period of CV cycling. With the exception of commercial RuO2 samples, the Tafel slopes was acquired for the other two electrode materials (CS, CSBGQD-13), as shown in Figure S17, and marginally declined from their initial values was observed. This behaviour demonstrated the unimpeded catalytic performances during the electrocatalysis and their chemical states were remained intact. The overall OER performances, stability and durability of the electrocatalysts were represented in Table 1. Thus, our catalyst CSBGQD-13 could produce OH- at cathode and O2 at anode from water with greater durability and efficiency, which shows potential for a dependable bifunctional electrocatalyst.

Bifunctional behavior of the catalysts: From the aforementioned illustration of catalytic behavior of the catalysts, CSBGQD-13 exerted to be a bifunctional electrode material over commercially available 10 % Pt/C as an ORR active only and RuO2 as an OER active only and the performance was shown in Table S7. This bifunctional behavior was well justified in Fig. 7f, where the current density obtained for CSBGQD-13 demonstrated effectiveness in both ORR and OER processes. The potential difference (∆E) between E1/2 (for ORR) and η10 mA/cm 2 (for OER) was obtained only 0.66 V vs. RHE during the LSV measurement within a wider potential window of 0–1.9 V vs. RHE in 0.1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. We believe the tandem structure of the composite which enhanced the electronic synergism between the two distinct interfaces (CS and BGQD) was responsible. This synergism is obtained from (a) the larger ECSA provided by a high conductive heterostructure with better mass transport efficiency and charge carrier transport rate (easy penetration of electrolyte into the catalyst layer),; (b) the facilitation of adsorption of O2 and OH- by offering a greater quantity of active catalytic sites; (c) the BGQD supported mixed valent Co-S surface favored the adsorption of oxygenated intermediates during the ORR/OER; (d) the doping of an electron deficient atom B into GQD, making a defect engineering system, which enabled electron transition as CS ↔ BGQD [49,60]. Thus, the strong electronic synergism coupled with chemical coupling and extraordinary stability makes our catalyst a superior candidate for energy generation.

Catalyst Recovery: Despite exceptional electrochemical oxygen electrolysis, it is necessary to note that catalyst recovery is a crucial component to understand about the catalyst surface reconstruction. As shown in Figure S18 and S16, HRTEM and XPS investigations were used to examine the catalyst's chemical composition and structural characteristics. Prior to and following 8000 CV cycles of ORR/OER procedures, as well as following 36 h chronoamperometric stability test, the TEM (Figure S18a) and HRTEM (Figure S18b and S15c) images of CSBGQD-13 showed essentially unchanged structural and chemical composition. From Figure S18c (i.e. the inverse FFT image of Figure S18b), it is clear that the coexistence of both the lattice planes for CS and BGQD. As seen in Figure S19, the XPS analysis revealed a reasonable shift in binding energy, especially for Co 2p (Figure S19a) and S 2p (Figure S19b) compared to the pre-electrolyte. This indicated a continuous interchange of redox active Co2+ ↔ Co3+ through S atom. However, Figure S19c showed a very small change in binding energy for B 1 s, suggesting that the BGQD defect sites remain unchanged. Therefore, the surface of the electrocatalyst remain largely unaltered with very minimum loss of surface reconstruction. This synergism may aid in improving our superior catalyst's adsorption/desorption kinetics and electron transfer kinetics with a higher E1/2, Eonset and lower overpotential. Therefore, each of the results offers a unique characteristic for creating an effective electrocatalyst with more active catalytic sites for oxygen electrode reactions, leading to a commendable and prospective bifunctional catalyst.

In the sluggish oxygen electrolysis material CoS, defect engineering B-doped GQD was integrated with it via a simple, cost-effective, easy-to-synthesize, facile hydrothermal method, to enhance the catalytic activities towards both ORR and OER. In this study, we brought together the electrode reactions (such as ORR and OER) of CSBGQD-13 nanohybrid in alkaline media because they may have a fundamental impact on microbial fuel cells, alkaline hydrogen fuel cells, and alkaline direct alcohol fuel cells, among other fuel cells. The excellent electrocatalysis was obtained by successfully loading of very low amount of B doping (less than 2 %, more precisely 1.5 %) into GQD matrix in CSBGQD-13 nanocomposite. A thorough caharacterizations were employed to understand their structural and chemical parameters via XRD, Raman, HRTEM and XPS analysis. With the help of BGQD, the electrical and geometric structure of CoS, particularly the defect engineering B-doping on its basal plane, provided higher catalytic active sites for oxygen electrolysis through faster electronic synergism between them. This synergism in the electronic structure offerred improved electrical conductivity, quicker electron transfer kinetics, higher ECSA and faster adsorption/desorption of oxygenated intermediates at the electrode surface. In a four-electron oxygen reduction process, CSBGQD-13 demonstrated exceptional ORR activity with high positive onset and half-wave potential that is comparable to that of commercial Pt/C catalysts.Therefore, the high limiting current density (7.6 mA/cm2 at 1600 rpm) in the wider potential range of 0–0.6 V vs. RHE with lower Tafel slope of 64 mV/dec, and high positive E1/2 (0.84 V vs. RHE) and a high resistance to the MeOH crossover effect of our composite electrode material makes it a very good candidate for the ORR processes. More importantly, with the lowest Tafel slope of 82 mV/dec among all CSBGQDs and a lower overpotential of 270 mV at a current density of 10 mA/cm2, CSBGQD-13 showed long-term durability and efficient OER activity in alkaline media. Additionally, with a very small ΔE value (only 0.66 V vs. RHE), a higher catalytic current density, a low Tafel slope with long-term stability CSBGQD-13 claims excellent dual function electrocatalytic performance towards ORR and OER. Therefore, we believe that this electrode material could offer a reliable grasp of the fundamentals of metal sulfide carbon dots hybrid catalysts for potential fuel cell applications.
